text:
The volume investigates the transnational aspects of unrest through the optic of perception and representation. Moving away from national history writing, the volume contributes to the growing field of transnational history by examining how revolts and rebellions were observed, interpreted and reported across the borders of states and cultures from late Medieval up to the eighteenth century. Through combining anthropological approaches with the history of communication and media, this volume offers a first ever attempt to look at the representation of unrest through the prism of the myriad reports and media of the times such as the eyewitness reports, travel accounts, diplomatic dispatches and imagery and visual media such as ballads and prints, or even-sheets and caricatures. These acts and representations of unrest contribute to what can be called a "connected history" of protest and rebellion and contribute to our understanding of early modern Europe from a new angle.

The contributions in this volume show how the representational landscape changed from the times of relative fewness of historical chronicles in the period of the Hundred Years War to the information-heavy propaganda wars in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. While tracing the numerous 'back-stories' of pretenders to the throne in Russia, the international alliances of the Ukrainian Cossacks and the Polish magnates as triggers for unrest in between the periods of foreign occupation, the volume focuses on the intercultural mechanisms which functioned as a catalyst for popular unrest in periods of institutional power vacuum. The foreign witnesses – unpunished by the ruling authorities as they were not nationals – were able to express the actors’ goals of the rebellions, swinging in their descriptions between labeling the rebels as mere 'scoundrels' or as justifiably oppressed victims of the king.

The second part of the volume focusses on the state's reaction to the revolutionary media and discusses whether or not the governments concerned had to engage in a counter-propaganda campaign in order to recapture the initiative. The state's attitude to foreign reporting changed significantly in Muscovy after the Strel’tsy uprising of 1698, as the Tsarist regime moved from complete indifference to vigorous coercion of undesirable reports on events at home in the Russian state. The third part is devoted to European pen- law and analyzes how politically motivated crime was codified and legally defined by different legal schools of European countries. All in all, the research projects presented in this volume demonstrate that the numerous and significant conflicts and rebellions which emerged during the pre-modern period throughout the European continent could by no means be regarded as isolated affairs that locally disturbed the political and social balance. Rather, they were milestones in a communicative process that spread not only within the confines of individual states but also beyond the borders of different regions and finally on a broad European-wide basis, giving rise to the formation of a European wide public sphere and a lexical, indeed grammatical, political vocabulary that described the fundamental opposing forces of civilization – order and disorder.
